title: Unit 28 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Idioms & Ancient Remnants

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 28: Idioms and Proverbs. This is the 'Soul' of the Greek language. You can know all the grammar in the world, but if you don't know why Greeks talk about 'eating wood' or 'losing their eggs', you'll never truly understand the culture. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why is Greek imagery so... vivid?
Γεια σας!
Greece is a land of food and history. We use what we know to describe what we feel. Our idioms are often funny, sometimes dark, but always very colorful.
Let's start with a classic one. 'Troo kseelo'. Literally... 'I am eating wood'.
Exactly.
Τρώω ξύλο.
It doesn't mean you are hungry for timber. It means you are getting a beating or being punished.
Θα φας ξύλο!
you're going to get it! It comes from the old times when punishments were... well... wooden.
I hope none of our listeners are 'eating wood'. What about when someone is talking too much? We say they 'ate our ears'?
Yes!
Μου έφαγε τα αυτιά.
He ate my ears. It means he talked my head off.
Σταμάτα πια, μου έφαγες τα αυτιά!
stop it already, you're talking too much!
And when someone is completely lost or confused? They lose their eggs and their baskets?
Χάνει τα αυγά και τα καλάθια.
To lose the eggs and the baskets. It means to be in a total mess or to lose control of a situation. It’s like everything is falling apart.
Now... proverbs. These are the life lessons passed down through generations. Athina, give us your favorite piece of advice.
This is a very famous one.
Σιγά σιγά, γίνεται η αγουρίδα μέλι.
Slowly, slowly, the sour grape becomes honey. Patience, practice, and time. That is the only way to master Greek.

'Sighá sighá'. From the sour grape to the honey. You have all put in the time, and you are tasting the honey now. This concludes Unit 28. You have the grammar, and now you have the soul. Excellent work.
Συγχαρητήρια σε όλους! Τα καταφέρατε!
This concludes our series. Thank you for joining us on this 'Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass'. Ya sas!
Γεια σας!
